reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author acknowledged that the key option for zswap writeback trigger is preferred over an explicit interface and explained its advantages in terms of future proofing.

Author acknowledged that future-proofing the zswap writeback trigger is necessary, considering potential additions/changes/new-features for this interface, such as triggering from a specific node or lowest memory tier. Author asked for input on whether zswap should be considered a top swap tier or something different.
reviews.raw_body:
+Youngjun

On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 11:30:30AM -0700, Shakeel Butt wrote:

---

Youngjun is working on swap tiers. At the moment he is more interested in
allowing a specific swap device to a memcg or not. I can imagine in future there
will be use-cases where there will be a need to demote data on higher tier swap
to lower tier swap. What would be the appropriate interface?

BTW does zswap folks think of zswap as a top swap tier or something different?

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that zswap writeback is similar to proactive reclaim and suggested applying the same semantics to memory.reclaim, including node-specific and age-based reclaim, which could be overloading a single interface

Reviewer expressed uncertainty about the placement of zswap in the swap hierarchy, considering it as a top swap tier but acknowledging potential future complexity with memory tiers and compressed memory nodes.
reviews.raw_body:
The way I see it, zswap writeback is just a "special" type of
proactive reclaim, but the goal is still proactively freeing cold
memory. In that regard, I think it makes sense to have things like
node-specific reclaim. Not sure about other extensions, but Hao
initially suggested making this age-based, so I think the answer is
yes.

For both of these examples (node-specific reclaim, age-based reclaim),
I think the same semantics could apply to memory.reclaim in general,
which is why I suggested making it a part of memory.reclaim. I also
like the idea of having a single proactive reclaim interface in
general, but maybe we don't want to overload it too much.

---

I haven't been following the swap tiers work closely, but personally I
do think of zswap as a top swap tier. Things will probably get more
blurry with memory tiers and compressed memory nodes though.

reviews.author: Shakeel Butt (author)
reviews.summary:
Author agrees that the key option for zswap writeback trigger is more clean and future-proof, but expresses concern about potential duplication in setting limits for swap tiers.

Author responded to Yosry Ahmed's feedback about the zswap writeback interface, stating that even if a key is used (zswap_writeback_only), there will still be a distinction between byte addressable and fault on access devices.
reviews.raw_body:
Same for me though I imagine swap tiers would introduce some duplication i.e.
different way (interface) to set limits for swap tiers for a given memcg.

---

I think there will still be distinction between byte addressable and fault on
access devices.

reviews.author: Yosry Ahmed
reviews.summary: Reviewer noted that the distinction between byte-addressable devices and fault-on-access devices is still maintained in this patch, but expressed concern about potential duplication of interface for setting limits on swap tiers within a given memcg.
reviews.raw_body:
Yeah, I think it makes sense to define "swap" as fault on access
(zswap, SSD, etc), and memory tiers as byte-addressable (even if you
put an SSD behind CXL and make it byte-addressable). But I also
remember seeing discussions about unifying memory tiers and swap in a
way, and it makes sense from a reclaim perspective (swap or demote
first?).

2026-06-08.patch_summary: This patch modifies the zswap shrink worker to writeback its cursor on a per-memcg basis. This change is necessary because proactive reclaim schemes can target cgroups and their children separately, but this can lead to inefficient reclaim if hotter memory in one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory in another cgroup is spared. The approach taken is to make the shrink worker's writeback cursor dependent on the memcg it is operating within.

reviews.author: YoungJun Park
reviews.summary:
Reviewer noted that swap tiers would introduce duplication of setting limits for a given memcg and requested a different interface

Reviewer YoungJun Park suggested integrating swap tiers into the existing zswap writeback interface, proposing to assign a target tier and allowing users to configure active tiers via memory.swap.tiers. He also proposed extending user-triggered demotion between swap tiers.

Reviewer YoungJun Park agreed that zswap should be defined as the default, top-most tier in swap_tier and referenced his own early RFC where he allocated a separate tier for zswap

Reviewer YoungJun Park noted that the proposed interface may introduce duplication with existing swap.max control, and suggested considering an alias for memory.zswap.writeback to set 'zswap only' as a tier
reviews.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 08, 2026 at 03:27:07PM -0700, Yosry Ahmed wrote:

+Chris +Kairui +Baoquan

Hello

Thanks for inviting me to the discussion, Shakeel.

---

Speaking of my work on swap tiers, I recently submitted a patch and am
currently considering memcg integration:
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260527062247.3440692-1-youngjun.park@lge.com/

The future use-cases imagined above seem to align with this
direction. (BTW, I am currently waiting for reviews/feedback from the memcg
folks on this patch. Any reviews would be highly appreciated!)

We could potentially assign a target tier
for writeback within the existing memory.zswap.writeback interface. 

For instance, '0' could mean disabled, while non-zero values could represent
specific tiers, which would maintain backward compatibility with the current
version. Alternatively, if zswap is treated as the default top tier, 
the `memory.swap.tiers` interface could potentially replace `memory.zswap.writeback`.

Furthermore, this could be expanded so that each swap tier can demote data
user-triggered demotion between swap tiers.

Based on the current patch's ideas combined with my swap tiers concept:

Assuming a hierarchy like:
zswap -> tier1 (SSD swap) -> tier2 (HDD swap) -> tier3 (Network swap)

We could configure the active tiers via a setting like `memory.swap.tiers`
(tier2 enabled, tier3 enabled).

For example, the concept of `echo "100M zswap_writeback_only > memory.reclaim"`
could be extended. A user could run `echo "100M tier2 > memory.reclaim"`
to explicitly trigger demotion from tier2 to tier3.
(BTW, if we combine these features, my personal preference for the keyword
format would be `<size> <demote_prefix><tier_name>`. I think it would be
better to explicitly indicate that it is a swap demotion by using a specific
prefix followed by the tier name. 
Or make demote prefix another key is also possible)

So, the whole picture would look something like this:
- memory.swap.tiers		: Interface for configuring the tier mask.
- memory.reclaim                : Entry point for user-triggered demotion.

---

Regarding zswap's position, I agree it needs to be defined as the default,
top-most tier in swap_tier. In my early RFC, I allocated a separate tier
specifically for zswap:
(https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20251109124947.1101520-3-youngjun.park@lge.com/)

---

I also agree with the concern about interface duplication. We will eventually
need a mechanism to control swap amounts per tier, which requires thinking
about its relationship with swap.max. (I raised this as an open question in
my early RFC).
https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20251109124947.1101520-1-youngjun.park@lge.com/
(Further Discussion and Open Questions Part)
However, since this feature is necessary anyway, wouldn't the proposed
interface be acceptable without causing conflicts at this early stage?

Additionally, `memory.zswap.writeback` seems redundant. Restricting a cgroup
to only use the zswap tier (assuming it's the first tier) is practically
identical to disabling `memory.zswap.writeback` (correct me if I'm wrong).
But there is no problem to integrate it as I think
e.g  `memory.zswap.writeback` could internally act as an alias for setting `memory.swap.tier` to 'zswap only'.

BR,
Youngjun Park

2026-06-09.patch_summary: This patch modifies the zswap shrink worker to writeback its cursor on a per-memcg basis. This change is necessary because proactive reclaim schemes can target cgroups and their children separately, but this can lead to inefficient reclaim if hotter memory in one cgroup is exhausted while colder memory in another cgroup is spared. The approach taken is to make the shrink worker's writeback cursor dependent on the memcg it is operating within.
